champion.narrative: Susan Innes (General Manager Supply Chain) is an active, engaged champion with prior Zycus experience and strong internal credibility. She drove the evaluation restart in May 2026 after joining Civeo, scoped the two demos (CLM-focused 9 Jun, sourcing-focused 25 Jun), requested ballpark pricing on 18 Jun, and on 6 Jul confirmed 'she will be in contact this week, next step is supposedly RFI between two vendors' (Next_Step__c 6 Jul 2026). Susan has direct Zycus experience from her prior roles at Stanwell and Aurizon ('Susan Innes (ex Stanwell), (ex Aurizon)' per Next_Step__c 16 Jun; she stated on 1 Jun prep call 'at Stanwell, I did use Zycus, so I do know the RFT side, the RFO, the quick source, all that sort of stuff capability, and I think they were looking at the risk module at a point in time'). This prior experience accelerates trust and shortens the learning curve—she is not evaluating cold, she is validating fit for Civeo's specific pain. She has access to the key stakeholders: she brought legal (Iona Borthwick, General Counsel) to the 9 Jun demo, IT (Tyler Scott, GM) to both demos, and procurement/ops stakeholders (Harvinder, Jodie, Max, Lananh) to the 25 Jun demo. She explicitly stated her role in the approval chain on 1 Jun: 'I'm in Sydney tomorrow with our IT general manager on another meeting for something else, so I will plant the seed with him tomorrow… he'll be my interface into IT and Canada because his director manages both countries,' and 'budgeting wise, having the two of us on the platform helps me get the economy of scale and the return on investment if I can sell it.' The phrase 'if I can sell it' confirms she is championing internally but does not hold final budget authority—she is building the business case to take to the CFO or COO. Her engagement is active and current: last buyer email received 30 Jun (6 days ago), and she updated Next_Step__c on 6 Jul (today) confirming this week's follow-up. Evidence of advocacy: she explicitly compared Zycus favorably to her Stanwell experience ('it's a lot more advanced, especially in that automation, prepopulation, AI piece') and proactively connected Tyler Scott to the eval ('I will plant the seed with him tomorrow'). The risk is that Susan is the single point of contact for procurement and legal, and the Canadian counterpart and Tyler's US-based director (who manages both countries) have not yet been engaged. If Susan cannot secure their buy-in, the deal could fragment (repeating the Bonfire failure where 'Canada moved forward with stuff and forget we're over here, and Australia went, well, that doesn't meet our purposes'). The next move is to reinforce Susan's champion role by co-developing the quantified ROI case with her in the next 7 days (so she has a CFO-ready business case to 'sell it'), and to ask her to facilitate an intro to the Canadian procurement lead and Tyler's US director within 14 days for a joint global-alignment session. Susan is strong, active, and credible—she is carrying the deal forward. The gap is not her strength, it is the missing economic buyer and Canadian stakeholder engagement above her.

competition.narrative: Coupa is the single named active competitor in a two-vendor RFI. Susan mentioned on 18 Jun (Next_Step__c) that Civeo 'Has mentioned also receiving demo and information from Coupa,' and on 6 Jul she confirmed 'next step is supposedly RFI between two vendors' (Coupa vs Zycus). No other competitor has been named across any call, field, task, or Next_Step. The incumbent state is manual/paper-based with no system (Susan: 'we have no system, everything's paper based' on 1 Jun call), so this is not a replacement deal—it is a first-time procurement platform buy. Canada uses Bonfire (a lighter sourcing tool) but Australia abandoned it after two years of non-adoption ('Australia had it licensed but no one used it when I got here. We paid two years worth of Bonfire that no one even picked up or rolled with, so I canceled it' per Susan, 1 Jun call). Bonfire is out of consideration—it is dead in Australia and Susan emphasized the need for global collaboration to avoid repeating the split decision. Coupa is the peer threat. Susan has Zycus experience from Stanwell/Aurizon and explicitly stated on 1 Jun 'I do know the RFT side, the RFO, the quick source capability' from her prior Zycus use, and praised the AI advancement ('it's a lot more advanced, especially in that automation, prepopulation, AI piece'). This gives Zycus a trust and familiarity advantage. However, one fit gap surfaced on the 25 Jun demo: Lananh (category manager) raised item-level pricing validation edge cases—'need a method to evaluate whether submitted products meet exact specifications and handle variations in submissions' and 'need to flag line-item price anomalies when supplier total matches spec but individual item prices diverge' (25 Jun notes). Zycus committed to a follow-up trial or detailed response, but if this is not resolved Coupa could exploit the gap by positioning deeper line-item analytics. Coupa's strengths are brand (well-known, large installed base) and potentially deeper line-item pricing analytics (if they have solved Lananh's edge case). Zycus's wedge is (1) Susan's prior positive experience and trust; (2) AI/automation depth (Merlin, AI-powered contract discovery, supplier risk monitoring)—Susan called this out as a differentiator; (3) integrated Source-to-Pay vs Coupa's more modular approach; (4) CLM + supplier onboarding fit (the 9 Jun demo landed well with legal and ops). The activation is to resolve Lananh's item-level pricing question within 7 days (trial or detailed design response), position Merlin AI as the differentiator (agentic sourcing, contract metadata extraction, supplier risk monitoring from public sources—capabilities Susan has not seen elsewhere), and co-develop the quantified ROI case with Susan so she can defend Zycus's value vs Coupa's likely lower price. Coupa will compete on price and brand; Zycus wins on AI depth, Susan's trust, and integrated fit if we close the Lananh gap and arm Susan with a quantified business case.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is acute, well-articulated, and owned by Susan Innes (GM Supply Chain) and Iona Borthwick (General Counsel). Susan described the current state on the 1 Jun prep call with surgical specificity: 'The biggest callout or key challenge for our department here at Civeo is we have no system. We're starting from ground zero. Everything's paper based. The only automation we have is DocuSign for the actual contracts. Everything else is stored in an O drive—we have a contract folder for every supplier. We have to manually track an Excel spreadsheet looking at expiries, all that jazz. Our certifications, insurances, etcetera, are currently being stored in our safety system, which isn't—it's disjointed. So none of that connects' (verbatim, 1 Jun 2026). She went on: 'Our legal counsel, they're in the same boat. They're managing procurement contracts and external facing client requirements. Right now, our legal counsel doesn't have visibility across her team's workload other than what comes in an inbox—a generic legal@CBO—and then her legal team will just grab it and work on something. So she doesn't have the ability to see who's at capacity, who's not, how can I reassign, and there's no—the other challenge, if you do mid-swap someone to another activity, they lose all that knowledge, and you gotta have a handover, you gotta waste time. Whereas if it's all in the system, it's everything's a one-stop shop, whoever takes over can see where it's at and what we've done' (verbatim, 1 Jun). She also highlighted supplier-onboarding pain: 'The moment, I don't have either. It's all—they connect with us, we send out a supplier pack, and it's all very manual' and 'Our safety system that we are storing in at the moment is not supported anymore… I've selfishly moved to ServiceNow because at minimum I can track that in ServiceNow until we get the contract management system' (1 Jun call). The pain is operational (manual, error-prone, no workflow), compliance (unsupported systems, no expiry alerts, governance risk), and capacity (legal has no visibility, procurement has no pipeline tracking). The pain owners are Susan (procurement/supply chain) and Iona (legal), both of whom attended the 9 Jun demo. The compelling event is implicit: the unsupported safety system and the Bonfire non-adoption history create urgency to get this decision right and implemented before year-end. Susan's statement 'from a governance and compliance perspective, it'll make me sleep a bit easier as well' (1 Jun call) confirms the personal/business risk she owns. The pain is confirmed, deeply felt, and driving the evaluation. What is missing is the quantification—no cycle-time baseline, no legal-workflow hour burn, no compliance incident cost. The next move is to co-develop a quantified pain model with Susan: 'You said legal has no workflow visibility and manual handovers waste time—how many contracts per month does legal handle, and how many hours per contract does the current manual process consume? Let's quantify the annual hour burn and the compliance risk so we can build the CFO-ready ROI case together.' This converts confirmed qualitative pain into a quantified business case the EB can defend.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are not yet discussed in detail, but two gates are visible: InfoSec review and ERP/data-sovereignty requirements. Tyler Scott (IT GM) asked on the 9 Jun demo 'Can I ask which AI are you using in the background?' (verbatim, 9 Jun notes), and the 9 Jun notes mention 'Requirement for strict data sovereignty, security controls and supplier offboarding processes to meet state government and Victoria Police standards' (sourced to the demo discussion). This signals InfoSec review will be a formal gate, and Civeo's work with government clients (state government, Victoria Police) imposes elevated security/compliance standards. However, no formal InfoSec questionnaire, security-review SLA, or timeline has been discussed, and no legal/procurement channel (MSA, SOW, who signs, paper ownership) has been mapped. Susan mentioned on 1 Jun that legal currently uses DocuSign ('The only automation we have is DocuSign for the actual contracts'), so e-signature is familiar, but whether Civeo will use their own paper or Zycus SaaS standard is unknown. The 25 Jun sourcing demo notes mention 'Integration and implementation: e-sign (DocuSign/Certinal) supported, bulk data migration/templates available, and Zycus will configure pricing templates and master-data mapping during implementation,' indicating implementation scoping is a known workstream but not yet detailed. No SOW, MSA timeline, or T4C/redline process has been discussed. The next move is to proactively surface the InfoSec review: send Tyler Scott (and Susan) the Zycus SOC 1/2 evidence pack, DPA/GDPR+TOM (including Zycus-India sub-processor disclosure), AI-governance materials (which AI models Zycus uses, data residency, AIGC board readiness), and the standard InfoSec questionnaire within 7 days, so the review can run in parallel with the RFI decision rather than becoming a post-selection bottleneck. Also ask Susan whether Civeo will use Zycus standard SaaS paper or require their own MSA, and who the legal signatory will be (Iona Borthwick, General Counsel, is the likely legal lead).

Susan Innes (GM Supply Chain) is the champion building the business case and stated 'budgeting wise, having the two of us on the platform helps me get the economy of scale and the return on investment if I can sell it' (1 Jun call)—the phrase 'if I can sell it' confirms she does not hold final budget authority. Tyler Scott (IT General Manager, based in Sydney, whose director 'manages both countries' and 'IT's global out of US', per 1 Jun call) attended both demos and is the interface to IT and Canada, but he is an influencer, not the budget owner. For a $250k global platform deal, the CFO or COO almost certainly must approve, and neither has been identified or engaged. Susan said on 1 Jun 'I'm in Sydney tomorrow with our IT general manager on another meeting for something else, so I will plant the seed with him tomorrow… he'll be my interface into IT and Canada because his director manages both countries,' indicating the approval chain runs through Tyler's US-based director (IT) and likely the CFO. The 9 Jun demo included 'General Manager for Business Applications based in Sydney' (a fifth attendee, not Tyler), possibly another IT/finance stakeholder, but no CFO, COO, or C-level executive has appeared on any call or been named in contact roles. The evidence shows Susan is championing internally but the power seat is empty. The next move is to ask Susan directly: 'Who will approve the $250k budget—CFO, COO, or Tyler's US director? Can we brief them on the ROI case together in the next two weeks?' Without EB engagement before the RFI decision, the deal is at risk of stalling after vendor selection.

decision_process.narrative: The process Civeo is running is visible from the call sequence and Susan's updates, but the approval chain and timeline are not fully locked. The sequence so far: (1) initial discovery and demo request (cancelled 25 Jun 2025, rescheduled to 16 Jul 2025 reconnect—early touchpoints, no content); (2) formal engagement restart May 2026 when Susan joined Civeo (she has Stanwell/Aurizon Zycus experience); (3) 1 Jun 2026 prep call with Susan to scope priorities (CLM-first, supplier management, sourcing secondary); (4) 9 Jun 2026 deep-dive CLM demo with legal (Iona Borthwick, General Counsel, Senior Legal Counsel), procurement (Susan, Harvinder, category managers), IT (Tyler Scott, GM Business Applications), and supply chain ops—broad stakeholder validation; (5) 18 Jun 2026 ballpark pricing requested (Next_Step__c: 'Also requested ballpark pricing'); (6) 25 Jun 2026 sourcing deep-dive demo with procurement and ops stakeholders (Harvinder, Jodie, Max, Lananh, Susan, Tyler, Christine); (7) 6 Jul 2026 (today) Susan updated Next_Step__c: 'Second deep dive completed, Susan advises she will be in contact this week, next step is supposedly RFI between two vendors' (Coupa vs Zycus). The approval chain is implied but not confirmed: Susan stated on 1 Jun 'I will plant the seed with Tyler tomorrow… he'll be my interface into IT and Canada because his director manages both countries. So IT's global out of US,' and 'budgeting wise, having the two of us on the platform helps me get the economy of scale and the return on investment if I can sell it.' This indicates a multi-level approval: (1) Susan champions internally; (2) Tyler Scott (IT GM) interfaces to his US-based director (IT/Canada); (3) likely CFO or COO signs off on $250k global spend. The 1 Jun call also revealed 'Canada do have a system—they're using Bonfire. Australia had it licensed but no one used it when I got here. We paid two years worth of Bonfire that no one even picked up or rolled with, so I canceled it… they moved forward with stuff and forget we're over here, and they picked the solution without collaboration, and then Australia went, well, that doesn't meet our purposes' (verbatim). Susan emphasized 'doing the collaboration with the relevant stakeholder is important so that we don't—we get everyone adopting it, not just part of the business.' This history creates a de-facto gate: Canadian stakeholder buy-in is required to avoid repeating the Bonfire non-adoption. Tyler's US director (who oversees both countries) and the Canadian procurement counterpart must be engaged before contract. No formal RFI timeline, vendor-selection date, or contract-signing milestone has been stated—Susan's 6 Jul note says 'supposedly RFI between two vendors' (tentative language). The next move is to ask Susan for the RFI timeline, the approval chain (CFO? COO? Tyler's director?), and whether the Canadian procurement lead and Tyler's US director can join a joint session in the next 14 days to align on global requirements and avoid a repeat of the Bonfire split decision.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are explicitly confirmed across the two demos and Susan's articulated priorities. On the 1 Jun prep call Susan stated the evaluation priorities: (1) contract lifecycle management (CLM) first—'real focus on how legal can see team workflow, reassign workload, manage both internal procurement contracts and client-facing contracts with role-based confidentiality'; (2) supplier onboarding and certificate/insurance management—'I'd love somewhere suppliers can register, we have a pool, and we track certifications and insurances in one space with automated expiry alerts'; (3) sourcing capability as secondary—'if we have time, touch on sourcing; happy to do a separate session if needed, I'd rather focus on CLM and supplier management first' (verbatim, 1 Jun call). The 9 Jun demo focused on CLM (contract authoring, redlining, metadata extraction, Word plugin, role-based security, legal workflow visibility) and the 25 Jun demo focused on sourcing (supplier onboarding workflow, risk/performance scorecards, strategic and tactical sourcing templates, question imports, negotiation rounds). Susan confirmed the CLM fit on 1 Jun ('I do know the RFT side, the RFO, the quick source capability from my Stanwell days, so definitely aware') and called out AI automation as a key differentiator ('Andre introduced it, it's a lot more advanced, especially in that automation, prepopulation, AI piece'). However, one fit gap surfaced on the 25 Jun call: Lananh (category manager) raised item-level pricing validation edge cases—'need a method to evaluate whether submitted products meet exact specifications and handle variations in submissions' and 'need to flag line-item price anomalies when supplier total matches spec but individual item prices diverge' (25 Jun notes). Zycus committed to a follow-up trial or detailed response. This is a tactical fit question, not a showstopper, but if left unresolved Coupa could exploit it. The criteria Civeo is evaluating on are: CLM + legal workflow automation (must-have), supplier onboarding + compliance tracking (must-have), AI/automation depth (differentiator), item-level pricing validation (Lananh's edge case, pending response), and global alignment (Australia + Canada on one platform).

cro_panel.intro: Zycus is the trusted finalist in a two-vendor RFI (vs Coupa) with a strong champion (Susan, prior Stanwell/Aurizon Zycus user), multi-threaded stakeholder validation (legal, IT, procurement, ops), and clear fit on CLM and supplier management; but Economic Buyer is unmapped, Canadian stakeholders are not engaged, no quantified ROI case has been delivered, and Lananh's 25 Jun item-level pricing validation question is unresolved.; The deal is live (Susan confirmed 6 Jul 'she will be in contact this week, next step is supposedly RFI'), but the next 14 days are critical; resolve Lananh's fit gap, arm Susan with a CFO-ready ROI case, map and brief the Economic Buyer, deliver InfoSec materials to Tyler, and lock Canadian buy-in via a joint global session; or risk losing to Coupa on price or stalling at budget approval after RFI decision

competitive_position.summary: Two-vendor RFI: Coupa vs Zycus. Coupa is the single named active competitor (mentioned 18 Jun, RFI confirmed 6 Jul). No other rival named. Incumbent state is manual/paper-based (no system); Canada uses Bonfire but Australia abandoned it after non-adoption. Zycus holds trust advantage (Susan's positive Stanwell/Aurizon experience, AI depth), but one fit gap (Lananh's 25 Jun item-level pricing validation edge case) is unresolved and could be Coupa's wedge. Current threat: Coupa is medium (active RFI finalist, brand strength, possible line-item analytics depth) but Zycus is positioned to win if we resolve Lananh's question within 7 days and arm Susan with a quantified ROI case.

Susan emphasized on 1 Jun that Bonfire failed because 'Canada moved forward and Australia was left out'—if Canadian stakeholders are not aligned before contract, deal could fragment at global approval.

Susan explicitly praised Zycus's AI advancement on 1 Jun ('it's a lot more advanced, especially in that automation, prepopulation, AI piece for stakeholders') and validated prior positive experience from Stanwell/Aurizon—champion is credible, advocating internally, and accelerating trust.

No quantified ROI case delivered yet. Business problem is deeply confirmed (manual tracking, no workflow visibility, compliance risk, unsupported systems) but not quantified—no cycle-time baseline, no legal-workflow hour savings, no compliance cost-avoidance figure. Without a quantified business case, approval at the budget-holder level (CFO or COO) will stall.

Susan Innes (champion, GM Supply Chain) is building the business case but stated 'if I can sell it' (1 Jun call), confirming she must present upward for approval. Tyler Scott (IT GM) is a technical validator, not the budget holder. Final approval for a $250k global SaaS platform almost certainly requires CFO or COO sign-off; neither has been named or invited to a call. Without the budget authority mapped and engaged, this deal cannot progress past proposal to negotiation. Recommended move: deal owner's manager to engage Susan, map the approval chain, and request an executive briefing or ROI workshop that puts the business case in front of the decision-maker, with Susan and Tyler sponsoring it internally.
